title: Paycheck Fairness Act
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Refor
plain_summary: This bill makes new rules to help make sure men and women get paid the same amount for doing the same job. It stops bosses from punishing workers who talk about how much money they make with their coworkers. The bill also says companies cannot ask job applicants how much money they made at their last job. Companies will have to keep track of how much they pay different workers and share this information with the government to make sure they are being fair.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for c

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Paycheck Fairness Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Enhanced Enforcement of Equal Pay Requirements
sections.text:
This section changes how the Fair Labor Standards Act defines sex. Sex now includes pregnancy, childbirth, or related medical conditions. It also includes sexual orientation (like being gay, straight, or bisexual) and gender identity (how someone identifies their gender, regardless of the sex they were assigned at birth). Sex also includes physical sex characteristics, including intersex traits.

The section changes the rules about when employers can pay different wages. Employers can only pay different wages if they can prove the difference is based on something real and job-related other than sex. This could be education, training, or experience. The employer must show that this factor is not based on sex differences in pay, is actually needed for the job, serves a real business need, and explains the entire pay difference. If a worker can show there is another way to do business that would not create the pay difference, and the employer refuses to use that other way, then the employer cannot use this defense.

Employees are considered to work in the same workplace if they work for the same employer in locations within the same county or similar local area. This definition can be broader based on rules from the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission.

The section makes it illegal for employers to punish workers who file complaints about pay discrimination, oppose unfair pay practices, or ask about or discuss wages. Employers cannot make workers sign contracts that stop them from talking about their wages.

There is an exception for employees whose job requires them to access other workers' wage information. These employees cannot share wage information with people who do not normally have access to it, unless they are responding to a complaint or helping with an investigation.

If an employer breaks the equal pay rules, they must pay extra damages to make up for the harm caused. If the employer acted with malice or was recklessly careless, they may also have to pay punitive damages (extra money to punish bad behavior). The federal government cannot be forced to pay punitive damages.

Workers can join together in class action lawsuits to enforce equal pay rules. Courts can order employers to pay the workers' attorney fees, including expert witness fees.

The Secretary of Labor can also take legal action against employers who violate equal pay rules and seek the same types of damages.

The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission will be responsible for enforcing equal pay rules. The Secretary of Labor can also enforce these rules for federal contractors through the Office of Federal Contract Compliance Programs. Both agencies must work together and create rules to explain how these laws work.

sections.title: Section 3 Training
sections.text: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission and the Secretary of Labor will provide training to their employees and to affected people and organizations about wage discrimination, if Congress provides money for this training.

sections.title: Section 4 Negotiation Skills Training
sections.text:
The Secretary of Labor will create a program to give contracts and grants for training employers about how salary negotiation and inconsistent wage practices can allow bias to affect compensation. The training will cover how structural issues may cause unfair earning and advancement opportunities for women and people of color. Topics include self-auditing to find bias in compensation, recruiting diverse job candidates, salary negotiations that result in similar workers getting different starting pay, making sure workers with similar jobs get similar pay, using consistent market rates, evaluating how fast employees advance to higher paying jobs, work assignments that create advancement opportunities, training and promotion opportunities, the impact of hiring senior workers compared to existing worker wages, opportunities for commissions and bonuses, performance reviews and raises, and processes for fixing inconsistent and unfair compensation.

The Secretary of Labor can also create a grant program to fund negotiation skills training programs that address pay differences, including outreach to women and girls. Public agencies, nonprofit organizations, and community-based organizations can apply for these grants.

The Secretary of Labor and Secretary of Education must create rules to include negotiation skills training in existing education and workforce programs where practical.

The Secretary of Labor must report to Congress every year about these activities and how well they work to achieve their goals.

sections.title: Section 5 Research, Education, and Outreach
sections.text:
The Secretary of Labor must study and provide information to employers, unions, and the public about ways to eliminate pay differences between men and women, including women from different racial and ethnic backgrounds. This includes conducting research to find ways to fix conditions that cause pay differences, publishing findings and materials about eliminating pay differences, supporting local educational programs, providing information to employers and organizations about eliminating pay differences, and recognizing employers, unions, and professional associations that have worked to eliminate pay differences.

The Secretary of Labor must also conduct research on the gender pay gap among younger workers. Within twelve months of this law passing, the Department of Labor will review existing research on wage gaps among young workers and make this information available on their website. Within thirty-six months, they will request proposals and fund new studies on this topic and make those studies available on their website.

sections.title: Section 6 Establishment of the National Award for Pay Equity in the Workplace
sections.text: This section creates the National Award for Pay Equity in the Workplace. The Secretary of Labor and the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission will give this award every year to an employer who has made significant efforts to follow equal pay laws. The Secretary of Labor and the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission will set the requirements for winning this award and create procedures for applying and presenting it. To qualify, an employer must have made substantial efforts to eliminate pay differences between men and women and deserve special recognition for this effort. The term employer includes corporations, partnerships, professional associations, unions, and similar business organizations. It also includes entities that run education, training, or apprenticeship programs, and joint programs formed by combinations of these organizations.

sections.title: Section 7 Collection of Pay Information by the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission
sections.text:
Within twenty-four months of this law passing, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission must collect compensation data from employers every year. This data must be separated by the sex, race, and national origin of employees. The Commission may also require other employment-related data like hiring, firing, and promotion information separated by these categories. The Commission's main goal is to find the most effective way to enforce federal laws that prohibit pay discrimination. They must also consider the burden on employers, how often reports are required, protecting data privacy, and the best format for reporting.

For each twelve-month period, the data must include compensation information separated by race, national origin, sex, and job categories. The Commission can use pay ranges showing how many employees earn compensation within certain ranges and the total hours worked by those employees. The Commission can adjust these pay ranges if needed to better enforce anti-discrimination laws or to account for inflation.

For employees who are not required to get overtime pay, employers can report either standard hours (forty hours per week for full-time, twenty hours for part-time) or actual hours worked. The Commission will use this data to better investigate discrimination complaints and allocate resources for investigations. Every eighteen months, the Commission will make summary compensation data available to the public, organized by industry, occupation, and geographic area.

This data collection applies to private employers with one hundred or more employees, including federal contractors and subcontractors, or other employers the Commission determines appropriate.

sections.title: Section 8 Reinstatement of Pay Equity Programs and Pay Equity Data Collection
sections.text: The Bureau of Labor Statistics must continue collecting data on women workers in their employment surveys. The Office of Federal Contract Compliance Programs must collect compensation and other employment data organized by demographics and require at least half of all non-construction contractors each year to prepare and file this data. They must review and use this data to identify contractors for further evaluation and enforcement. The Department of Labor must make accurate information about compensation discrimination easily available in print, on their website, and through other channels. This includes statistics, explanations of employee rights, historical analyses, instructions for employers on compliance, and other information to help the public understand and address discrimination.

sections.title: Section 9 Prohibitions Relating to Prospective Employees' Salary and Benefit History
sections.text:
This section makes it illegal for employers to use a job applicant's wage history in considering them for employment, including requiring that previous wages meet certain minimum or maximum requirements. Employers cannot use wage history to determine wages for a prospective employee, except if the applicant voluntarily provides wage history after receiving a job offer with compensation to support a higher wage than what was offered. Employers cannot ask job applicants or their current or former employers about wage history, except to confirm wage information only after making a job offer and only if the applicant provides previous wage information to support a higher wage. Employers cannot fire or retaliate against employees or job applicants for opposing these practices or taking action against wage discrimination. Wage history means the wages paid to the prospective employee by their current or previous employer.

Employers who violate these rules face a civil penalty of five thousand dollars for a first offense, increasing by one thousand dollars for each additional offense, up to ten thousand dollars maximum. They must also pay each affected employee or job applicant up to ten thousand dollars in special damages plus attorney fees. Courts can also order employers to stop the illegal behavior. Workers or job applicants can sue in federal or state court individually or as a group to recover these damages.

sections.title: Section 10 National Equal Pay Enforcement Task Force
sections.text: This section creates the National Equal Pay Enforcement Task Force with representatives from the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, the Department of Justice, the Department of Labor, and the Office of Personnel Management. The task force will improve compliance, public education, and enforcement of equal pay laws by making sure these agencies coordinate their efforts and reduce gaps in enforcement. The task force will investigate challenges related to pay inequality, develop recommendations to address those challenges, and create action plans to implement the recommendations.

sections.title: Section 11 Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: Congress can provide whatever money is necessary to carry out this law. None of the money provided for the grant program in section four can be used for congressional earmarks as defined in House rules.

sections.title: Section 12 Small Business Assistance
sections.text: This law takes effect six months after it is signed. The Secretary of Labor and the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission must work together to create technical assistance materials to help small businesses comply with this law. Small businesses are exempt from this law to the same extent they are exempt from the Fair Labor Standards Act.

sections.title: Section 13 Notice Requirements
sections.text: Every employer must post and keep posted notices about the requirements of this law in obvious places where employee notices are usually posted. The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission and Secretary of Labor will prepare or approve these notices. The notice can be combined with other required notices. Employers must also post electronic copies of the notice on internal websites that employees can access and tell employees on the website where the physical notice is posted.

sections.title: Section 14 Rule of Construction
sections.text: Nothing in this law affects the obligation of employers and employees to fully comply with all immigration laws, including being subject to penalties, fines, or other punishments.

sections.title: Section 15 Severability
sections.text: If any part of this law or its application to certain people or situations is found invalid or unconstitutional, the rest of the law and its application to other people or situations will not be affected.
